hypothesis: The bucketed two-stage soft-codebook backbone that now wins from K=24 through K=64 under EF=16 may also overtake the old high-K routed anchor at K=96, indicating that bucketed locality remains beneficial even in the higher-capacity regime.
summary: After four consecutive frontier improvements at K=16, K=24, K=32, and K=64 with the same architecture and recipe, the highest-value next step is to test K=96 while holding everything else fixed. This directly maps whether the bucketed family can extend its EF=16 advantage into the high-K lane rather than remaining only a low-to-mid-K winner.
self_review: This is the cleanest continuation of the current win streak because it changes only K and probes the next frontier-relevant anchor above the new K=64 point. The main risk is that bucketing may become too restrictive at high K, but K=96 is exactly the right test for locating that transition.
notes_to_memory: Round 129 continues systematic K-axis mapping of the bucketed family after frontier wins at K=16, K=24, K=32, and K=64 under EF=16.
Hold architecture and recipe fixed so the result isolates whether the bucketed advantage extends into the higher-K regime.
A strong K=96 result would make the bucketed family a serious candidate for the dominant EF=16 backbone across most of the explored Pareto surface.
next_hypotheses: If K=96 is competitive, test K=128 next to determine whether the bucketed family can replace the old routed high-K anchor entirely.
If K=96 regresses while K<=64 remain strong, treat the bucketed family as winning the low-to-mid-K lane but not the extreme high-K regime.
If K=96 is close to the old anchor but not clearly better, run one confirmatory repeat before promoting the bucketed family as the default EF=16 high-K backbone.
